skill.name: action-item-organizer
skill.description: Extracts actionable tasks from reports, meeting notes, reviews, and audits, then organizes them into prioritized markdown checklists with context and tracking metadata.
skill.summary: Extracts action items into prioritized markdown checklists.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 143 hits from markdown backticks, security vocabulary, and example credential text. Human review found documentation and examples only, with no executable scripts, network calls, environment reads, shell execution, or prompt injection language. The skill is safe to publish as a documentation-only productivity skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Formatting
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged markdown code fences and inline path examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The referenced content is instructional formatting for checklists and metadata examples, not executable commands or scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are markdown-only content, and the referenced backticks delimit examples or inline labels. No shell invocation or runtime execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Credential Terms in Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged API key, SECRET_KEY, and credential language as environment or secret access. These strings appear inside sample source documents and checklist examples that teach extraction, with no code that reads or transmits secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are examples of issues users might convert into tasks. I found no process.env, ENV access, network call, or executable script tied to these examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security Keywords in Priority Guidance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography and reconnaissance-related terms because the skill discusses security issue classification. The content explains how to prioritize findings and does not implement cryptography, scanning, or reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are priority criteria and example vulnerability labels. They describe how to organize tasks and do not contain operational security tooling or harmful instructions.

content.user_title: Organize Action Items Into Prioritized Checklists
content.value_statement: Important tasks are often scattered across meeting notes, reviews, and reports. This skill converts source material into prioritized markdown checklists with owners, context, estimates, and tracking details.
actual_capabilities: Extracts action items from reports, meeting notes, code reviews, audits, and planning documents.
Classifies tasks into P0, P1, P2, and P3 priority groups using documented criteria.
Preserves metadata such as source locations, owners, estimates, issue IDs, and context.
Builds nested markdown checklists with priority summaries and completion tracking.
Marks missing or ambiguous details with TBD notes for later review.
limitations: Does not execute tasks or update external project management systems by itself.
Priority and estimate accuracy depends on the quality of the source document.
Cannot verify referenced files, tickets, or owners unless that evidence is provided.
Works best with text documents; images and diagrams need separate transcription.

use_cases.title: Code Review Remediation
use_cases.description: Convert review comments and findings into a prioritized TODO list with files, owners, and implementation context.

use_cases.title: Meeting Follow-Up Tracking
use_cases.description: Extract scattered decisions and commitments from meeting notes into a clear checklist grouped by urgency.

use_cases.title: Audit Action Planning
use_cases.description: Turn audit findings into remediation tasks with risk-based priority, context, and traceable source references.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Basic Action Items
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for meeting notes or short documents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use action-item-organizer on these notes. Create a prioritized markdown checklist. Preserve owners, due dates, and context when present. Notes: [paste notes]

prompt_templates.title: Organize Code Review Findings
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for engineering review output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this code review report into a TODO list. Group by P0 through P3. Include file paths, owners, estimates, and rationale when available. Report: [paste report]

prompt_templates.title: Merge Multiple Sources
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for combining notes, issues, and plans.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these source documents together. Deduplicate repeated tasks, preserve source references, and produce one prioritized checklist with nested subtasks. Sources: [paste sources]

prompt_templates.title: Create Audit Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for risk-based remediation planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Transform this audit report into a remediation checklist. Map risk levels to P0 through P3, include impact context, mark missing metadata as TBD, and add completion tracking. Audit: [paste audit]

output_examples.input: Meeting notes say Sarah will update onboarding docs before launch, and Mike must fix the deployment script urgently.
output_examples.output: P0: Fix broken deployment script. Owner: Mike. Context: Urgent deployment blocker. P1: Update onboarding docs. Owner: Sarah. Context: Needed before launch.

output_examples.input: A review report lists missing authentication on an API route and a suggestion to improve variable names.
output_examples.output: P0: Add authentication to the API route with security context. P3: Improve variable names as a low-priority maintainability task.

output_examples.input: An audit report includes high, medium, and low findings with owners and estimates.
output_examples.output: A markdown checklist grouped by P0, P1, P2, and P3, with owner, estimate, source location, and completion tracking for each item.

best_practices: Provide the full source document so context, owners, and priorities can be preserved.
Review inferred priorities before using the checklist for sprint or release planning.
Keep source references in each task so teams can trace decisions back to the original document.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a flat list when the source contains complex tasks with subtasks.
Do not remove context or rationale, because task owners need to understand the impact.
Do not treat inferred estimates or priorities as final without team review.

faq.question: What documents work best with this skill?
faq.answer: It works best with text reports, meeting notes, review summaries, audit findings, project plans, and issue lists.

faq.question: Can it create nested tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It can turn complex work into parent tasks, subtasks, and detailed implementation steps.

faq.question: How does it choose priorities?
faq.answer: It uses P0 through P3 criteria based on urgency, impact, security risk, blockers, and source priority labels.

faq.question: Does it update Jira, GitHub Issues, or Asana?
faq.answer: No. It produces structured markdown that can be copied into external tools if needed.

faq.question: What happens when information is missing?
faq.answer: It marks missing owners, files, estimates, or priorities as TBD and adds notes for review.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with sensitive documents?
faq.answer: The skill itself is markdown guidance and does not include code for network access, shell execution, or secret collection.
